messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: i'm reaching the point where my BBS code is nearing ready to use, in an MVP sense -- it's to the point where i can run it for multiple days, and access it from a zero-hop node, but it's not quite ready for proper deployment yet. it's written in Python using meshcore_py, and currently works using a USB companion node as a "modem" where each DM session is a separate BBS connection. it's targeted for a Raspberry Pi Zero and an nRF based radio, so it can be solar powered. it's intentionally old-school, both for nostalgia reasons, and because it suits LoRa data sizes quite well. currently it supports an arbitrary number of rooms (discussion areas), private messages, and a simple privilege system. data is stored in a SQLite3 database. it's still missing many of the commands needed to be a truly functional system, while i work on making the MeshCore components work better in the real world. you can see the current state of development at https://github.com/taedryn/mesh-citadel . you can read more about Citadel BBSes via your favorite search engine, but they have a long history, the first system coming up in 1981 or so.
messages.content_html: i'm reaching the point where my BBS code is nearing ready to use, in an MVP sense -- it's to the point where i can run it for multiple days, and access it from a zero-hop node, but it's not quite ready for proper deployment yet. it's written in Python using meshcore_py, and currently works using a USB companion node as a "modem" where each DM session is a separate BBS connection. it's targeted for a Raspberry Pi Zero and an nRF based radio, so it can be solar powered. it's intentionally old-school, both for nostalgia reasons, and because it suits LoRa data sizes quite well. currently it supports an arbitrary number of rooms (discussion areas), private messages, and a simple privilege system. data is stored in a SQLite3 database. it's still missing many of the commands needed to be a truly functional system, while i work on making the MeshCore components work better in the real world. you can see the current state of development at https://github.com/taedryn/mesh-citadel. you can read more about Citadel BBSes via your favorite search engine, but they have a long history, the first system coming up in 1981 or so.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: i've been able to communicate from downtown seattle to my BBS (a multi-hop journey across a marginal connection). it was only a pair of commands, but they worked. one even worked after failing to get an ack back to me. good progress, which apparently was made possible by increasing the delay time to 6 seconds between command reception and sending the response. (edited)
messages.content_html: i've been able to communicate from downtown seattle to my BBS (a multi-hop journey across a marginal connection). it was only a pair of commands, but they worked. one even worked after failing to get an ack back to me. good progress, which apparently was made possible by increasing the delay time to 6 seconds between command reception and sending the response. (edited)

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: i think i figured out the problem -- the contact handling code wasn't correctly dealing with new adverts at all, so they never got saved to the DB or the node's memory. i've fixed that, so you might check out the code now, which is rapidly approaching actually-useful.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: i've just spent the evening tracking it down. it's been freezing for me too. i think i've figured it out, please pull the latest code and give it a try. if you want far too much verbosity in your logs, run it in debug mode (with -d). there was an error in the way automatic logouts were being handled that resulted in the whole system grinding to a halt, which i think i've fixed. you can test it by temporarily running with auth.session_timeout set to a very short time in your config.yaml file (i used 15 seconds), the error appeared to be a user would log in, the automatic logout would run 15 seconds later, and after this happened a time or two, everything locked up. this doesn't seem to be happening any more, but i'd love to hear that it's working for others as well. definitely don't leave auth.session_timeout set to a small value or users will have a bad time, but it's good if you want to test this particular bug.
messages.content_html: i've just spent the evening tracking it down. it's been freezing for me too. i think i've figured it out, please pull the latest code and give it a try. if you want far too much verbosity in your logs, run it in debug mode (with -d). there was an error in the way automatic logouts were being handled that resulted in the whole system grinding to a halt, which i think i've fixed. you can test it by temporarily running with auth.session_timeout set to a very short time in your config.yaml file (i used 15 seconds), the error appeared to be a user would log in, the automatic logout would run 15 seconds later, and after this happened a time or two, everything locked up. this doesn't seem to be happening any more, but i'd love to hear that it's working for others as well. definitely don't leave auth.session_timeout set to a small value or users will have a bad time, but it's good if you want to test this particular bug.

messages.author: FAte_CzE
messages.text: I see that a new user gets permission level 0 after registration, but the rooms are set to 1+ by default. Does that mean a new user can't access anything? I also tried the V (validate user) option, but it just tells me there are no users to validate.
messages.content_html: I see that a new user gets permission level 0 after registration, but the rooms are set to 1+ by default. Does that mean a new user can't access anything? I also tried the V (validate user) option, but it just tells me there are no users to validate.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: Yes, new users get level 0, but as soon as you validate them, they get level 1. There's not currently a way to elevate aides or sysops in the BBS interface, though you could do it in the database by changing their permission level in the users table. (edited)
messages.content_html: Yes, new users get level 0, but as soon as you validate them, they get level 1. There's not currently a way to elevate aides or sysops in the BBS interface, though you could do it in the database by changing their permission level in the users table. (edited)

messages.author: FAte_CzE
messages.text: Ok, so the new issue is that new users always start with permission level 0, but they don’t show up for validation until I manually set them to permission 2 for example. Then I can validate them, but they automatically drop back to 0 after validation, and I have to raise their permissions back to 2 again.
messages.content_html: Ok, so the new issue is that new users always start with permission level 0, but they don’t show up for validation until I manually set them to permission 2 for example. Then I can validate them, but they automatically drop back to 0 after validation, and I have to raise their permissions back to 2 again.

messages.author: FAte_CzE
messages.text: During today’s testing some regular text messages from clients were received as bool instead of plain text. The transport parser treated them as FromUserType.COMMAND, causing: Expected BaseCommand object, got <class 'bool'>, This breaks the login/registration flow and prevents prompts from being delivered.
messages.content_html: During today’s testing some regular text messages from clients were received as bool instead of plain text. The transport parser treated them as FromUserType.COMMAND, causing: Expected BaseCommand object, got <class 'bool'>, This breaks the login/registration flow and prevents prompts from being delivered.

messages.author: FAte_CzE
messages.text: Today's testing shows that the this issue occurs during active communication. The logs repeatedly show retry sending msg → resetting path → command_error (error_code: 1) → Failed to send, and this happens right at the start of an interaction after login. The BBS does have the contact stored, but at the moment of sending it no longer has a valid route to the recipient. When the route fails, the path reset also doesn't recover it, and the message exchange breaks down.
messages.content_html: Today's testing shows that the this issue occurs during active communication. The logs repeatedly show retry sending msg → resetting path → command_error (error_code: 1) → Failed to send, and this happens right at the start of an interaction after login. The BBS does have the contact stored, but at the moment of sending it no longer has a valid route to the recipient. When the route fails, the path reset also doesn't recover it, and the message exchange breaks down.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: I've seen that as well. I don't know how to make the BBS act better when it can't get a message through, though. What has been happening with me is that my RF path from the BBS to my repeater is excellent, but the path from my repeater to almost anywhere else is only good about 50% of the time, and it's better for some folks than others.
messages.content_html: I've seen that as well. I don't know how to make the BBS act better when it can't get a message through, though. What has been happening with me is that my RF path from the BBS to my repeater is excellent, but the path from my repeater to almost anywhere else is only good about 50% of the time, and it's better for some folks than others.

messages.author: FAte_CzE
messages.text: One thought: would it make sense to allow the BBS to designate a primary (or maybe secondary) routing peer? Basically a “backbone” node that it always tries first when sending messages. Right now the BBS has to discover routes dynamically each time, and when the path is unstable the retries/ack timing issues get amplified. If we could configure one preferred node to act as the first or second hop, it might greatly reduce the number of path resets and failed sends.
messages.content_html: One thought: would it make sense to allow the BBS to designate a primary (or maybe secondary) routing peer? Basically a “backbone” node that it always tries first when sending messages. Right now the BBS has to discover routes dynamically each time, and when the path is unstable the retries/ack timing issues get amplified. If we could configure one preferred node to act as the first or second hop, it might greatly reduce the number of path resets and failed sends.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: I like that thought. If the sysop configures a primary route, use, otherwise discover it. I'll have to see if MeshCore_py allows for that
messages.content_html: I like that thought. If the sysop configures a primary route, use, otherwise discover it. I'll have to see if MeshCore_py allows for that

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: new feature: a "stop" command to stop incoming messages. handy for rooms with lots of messages where you send the wrong command and find yourself faced with all the messages spaced at 5 seconds between 150 byte chunks.
messages.content_html: new feature: a "stop" command to stop incoming messages. handy for rooms with lots of messages where you send the wrong command and find yourself faced with all the messages spaced at 5 seconds between 150 byte chunks.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: for folks who've put up BBS instances, what's your success rate been with remote users? i'm having a miserable time at my location, but i mostly suspect poor RF path, as my DMs tend to be pretty miserable as well. i just want to make sure i haven't programmed in some kind of silly mistake that's making things work poorly.
messages.content_html: for folks who've put up BBS instances, what's your success rate been with remote users? i'm having a miserable time at my location, but i mostly suspect poor RF path, as my DMs tend to be pretty miserable as well. i just want to make sure i haven't programmed in some kind of silly mistake that's making things work poorly.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: i've posted an update that improves incoming packet de-duplication. i was not paying attention to timestamp, when i definitely should have been, for de-duplication. should provide a general improvement for users who don't have a perfect connection to the BBS.
messages.content_html: i've posted an update that improves incoming packet de-duplication. i was not paying attention to timestamp, when i definitely should have been, for de-duplication. should provide a general improvement for users who don't have a perfect connection to the BBS.

messages.author: FAte_CzE
messages.text: If the connection goes through a good route, it actually works quite reliably even over 2–3 hops. The issues appear on paths with only a decent SNR — there it often struggles to find a stable path, and duplicate messages start appearing, possibly because ACKs aren’t being received properly. I’ve just pulled the new version and shared it for testing — we’ll see if it improves things.
messages.content_html: If the connection goes through a good route, it actually works quite reliably even over 2–3 hops. The issues appear on paths with only a decent SNR — there it often struggles to find a stable path, and duplicate messages start appearing, possibly because ACKs aren’t being received properly. I’ve just pulled the new version and shared it for testing — we’ll see if it improves things.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: Thanks. I'll take any feedback you can think of with regard to improving packet handling (or anything else, but packet handling is the big pain point for me right now). No one but me has really been able to use my installation so far.
messages.content_html: Thanks. I'll take any feedback you can think of with regard to improving packet handling (or anything else, but packet handling is the big pain point for me right now). No one but me has really been able to use my installation so far.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: when you start up, are you seeing any error messages in the MeshCore initialization messages? there should be a whole bunch of them, then you should see that 2 transport engines have started, like this: [2025-11-15 10:38:52] INFO citadel.transport.engines.meshcore.meshcore_refactored: Connecting MeshCore transport at /dev/ttyACM0 [2025-11-15 10:38:52] INFO meshcore: Serial Connection started [2025-11-15 10:38:52] INFO citadel.transport.engines.meshcore.meshcore_refactored: Setting MeshCore node time to 1763231932 [... a bunch of settings messages ...] [2025-11-15 10:38:54] INFO citadel.transport.engines.meshcore.meshcore_refactored: Setting MeshCore multi-acks to 'True' [2025-11-15 10:38:54] INFO citadel.transport.engines.meshcore.meshcore_refactored: Ensuring contacts [2025-11-15 10:38:54] INFO citadel.transport.engines.meshcore.util: Sending advert (flood=False) [2025-11-15 10:38:55] INFO citadel.transport.engines.meshcore.contacts: Disabled meshcore auto-add of contacts [2025-11-15 10:38:55] INFO citadel.transport.engines.meshcore.contacts: ContactManager started with 84 cached contacts [2025-11-15 10:38:55] INFO citadel.transport.engines.meshcore.meshcore_refactored: MeshCore device connected [2025-11-15 10:38:55] INFO citadel.transport.manager: MeshCore engine started [2025-11-15 10:38:55] INFO citadel.transport.manager: Starting watchdog timer for MeshCore engine [2025-11-15 10:38:55] INFO citadel.transport.manager: Set watchdog timeout to 60s [2025-11-15 10:38:55] INFO citadel.transport.manager: Transport manager started with 2 engines running [2025-11-15 10:38:55] INFO citadel: Server running. Press Ctrl+C to shutdown. (edited)

Maybe it could help to log the inbound/outbound route that MeshCore is using for each message. That way we could actually see whether the chosen path makes sense. In my case the connected Companion can unfortunately hear a more distant repeater as well, and the BBS might sometimes pick that route even though it's not stable. I've tried TX power 1, 5, and also full power, with similar behavior.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: important update tonight: with the meshcore refactor, entering messages was broken. a fix for that went out a couple hours ago, plus a set of watchdog timers to hopefully deal with hardware errors like i've been seeing, where one or more of the listeners dies with an error that's not available to be handled in the current version of meshcore_py. it's definitely my hardware that's at fault, but this should restart the meshcore engine any time a meshcore event handler dies for whatever reason. (edited)
messages.content_html: important update tonight: with the meshcore refactor, entering messages was broken. a fix for that went out a couple hours ago, plus a set of watchdog timers to hopefully deal with hardware errors like i've been seeing, where one or more of the listeners dies with an error that's not available to be handled in the current version of meshcore_py. it's definitely my hardware that's at fault, but this should restart the meshcore engine any time a meshcore event handler dies for whatever reason. (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 2

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: yes, it's PNW, but my RF link is terrible, so getting through to my instance mostly doesn't work. there's a guide on how to do it here: https://github.com/taedryn/mesh-citadel/blob/main/README.md and you can look for "Tae's BBS Tacoma" as a running instance that's a bit more accessible. you can also try hitting my instance, "Tae's BBS", but be warned that my path back to the mesh is quite variable, and it's not likely to work well. (i'm working on it)

messages.author: FAte_CzE
messages.text: From our testing, it looks like the BBS really needs to wait longer for message delivery when the path goes over multiple hops. If the timeout is too short, the user quickly gets flooded with retry messages even though the packets may still be making their way through the mesh. Is the send_timeout parameter intended to control this behaviour? And if so, what would you recommend as a reasonable value for multi-hop routes?
messages.content_html: From our testing, it looks like the BBS really needs to wait longer for message delivery when the path goes over multiple hops. If the timeout is too short, the user quickly gets flooded with retry messages even though the packets may still be making their way through the mesh. Is the send_timeout parameter intended to control this behaviour? And if so, what would you recommend as a reasonable value for multi-hop routes?

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: i need to update the code, i realized last night that the meshcore firmware actually recommends a timeout time, and i should be paying attention to that instead of hard-coding the value as we have with the config file. i actually didn't realize it was timing out, i thought it was running out of retries, but i quite easily believe i was wrong.
messages.content_html: i need to update the code, i realized last night that the meshcore firmware actually recommends a timeout time, and i should be paying attention to that instead of hard-coding the value as we have with the config file. i actually didn't realize it was timing out, i thought it was running out of retries, but i quite easily believe i was wrong.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: tonight's push simplifies message sending a fair amount. turns out i was doing a lot of extra work that the meshcore_py library was already doing, and that doubled effort also probably means there were problems with ack receipt -- there were two handlers registered to receive and deal with ack packets, which may have been screwing things up. it also fixes another deadlock in session cleanup which will hard-stop the BBS process if a user loses their connection while attempting to register.
messages.content_html: tonight's push simplifies message sending a fair amount. turns out i was doing a lot of extra work that the meshcore_py library was already doing, and that doubled effort also probably means there were problems with ack receipt -- there were two handlers registered to receive and deal with ack packets, which may have been screwing things up. it also fixes another deadlock in session cleanup which will hard-stop the BBS process if a user loses their connection while attempting to register.
messages.reactions_html: 3

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: you'll need a computer (anything from a raspberry pi zero through to the latest and greatest, as long as it runs python), and a meshcore node loaded with the usb companion firmware. download the code from the github repo, and follow the directions in the README.md file for setting things up. it's not really ready for completely non-technical people to test yet, so the instructions assume you're pretty comfortable with the command line, the git command, and basic python tools.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: just pushed a new version. the only visible change is that the room creation process now logs the user who created the room, as it should have done from the start. but the big change is that the meshcore contacts submodule has been completely rewritten, and it makes a lot more sense now. it should (ideally) work exactly the same as it did before, but i got desperately sick of tracing through code that was a bit too vibe-coded for its own good.

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: it looks like the issue is in commit b0bb053 -- __init__.py was changed to import MeshCoreTransportEngine from main.py but no main.py was added that commit, and meanwhile the commit also deleted meshcore.py and meshcore_refactored.py which each contained that class prior

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: That looks like your BBS node doesn't have your public key on hand, so it can't decode the packet. Try advertising again, but it may also be a code change problem.
messages.content_html: That looks like your BBS node doesn't have your public key on hand, so it can't decode the packet. Try advertising again, but it may also be a code change problem.

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: My public key as in, the bbs nodes public key, or the connecting client's key?
messages.content_html: My public key as in, the bbs nodes public key, or the connecting client's key?

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: Both nodes need to have each other's public keys. They both have to advert, and save the resulting advert from the other. If you can start the DM from your personal node, you've already got the BBS's key. But it may be that your BBS's node doesn't have your personal node's key.
messages.content_html: Both nodes need to have each other's public keys. They both have to advert, and save the resulting advert from the other. If you can start the DM from your personal node, you've already got the BBS's key. But it may be that your BBS's node doesn't have your personal node's key.

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: ah, yep! That solved it, thanks.
messages.content_html: ah, yep! That solved it, thanks.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: Nice. Yeah, I started this project because I've always wanted to run a citadel again, but conditions didn't make sense. MeshCore is kind of an ideal BBS/phone line analogue for the modern era. I first started calling into BBSes in the early-mid 80s, and a lot of them were citadel.

MeshCore is kind of an ideal BBS/phone line analogue for the modern era. I first started calling into BBSes in the early-mid 80s, and a lot of them were citadel.

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: Forgive me if i'm telling you stuff you already know, but the way that meshcore radios work is that there is a "radio", and an "app". The radio and app communicate via something called "Companion Protocol." That protocol underneath is in fact "serial", but in order to interact with the radio the binary-based "Companion Protocol" is used to do that interaction. As part of the companion protocol, things like specified radio frequency, bandwidth, etc are available to be configured. So in this case "mesh-citadel" is an app that is utilizing a library that implements the Companion Protocol, and as such includes things like settable frequency as those are things that might need to be sent to a radio (for instance, if you plugged a fresh new meshcore device into it without configuring it first, citadel could theoretically handle all that setup for you based on the configuration values. Hope that helps!
messages.content_html: Forgive me if i'm telling you stuff you already know, but the way that meshcore radios work is that there is a "radio", and an "app". The radio and app communicate via something called "Companion Protocol." That protocol underneath is in fact "serial", but in order to interact with the radio the binary-based "Companion Protocol" is used to do that interaction. As part of the companion protocol, things like specified radio frequency, bandwidth, etc are available to be configured. So in this case "mesh-citadel" is an app that is utilizing a library that implements the Companion Protocol, and as such includes things like settable frequency as those are things that might need to be sent to a radio (for instance, if you plugged a fresh new meshcore device into it without configuring it first, citadel could theoretically handle all that setup for you based on the configuration values. Hope that helps!
